title: Talking about your past
setting: At dinner, Sara's father asks about your life before Lucerne.

steps.speaker: Vater
steps.es: Also Tom, erzäll: wohär chunsch und was häsch früener gmacht?
steps.en: So Tom, tell us: where are you from and what did you do before?

options.es: Ich bi z'Amerika ufgwachse und han als Ingenieur gschafft.
options.en: I grew up in America and worked as an engineer.
options.feedback: Clear past-tense narration — origin and work in one neat answer.
The facts are there but it's clipped. Full sentences invite warmth.
Leaves him hanging — he asked because he's interested. Give him a little.

steps.speaker: Vater
steps.es: Spannend! Und warum bisch grad uf Luzärn cho?
steps.en: Interesting! And why did you come to Lucerne of all places?

options.es: Wäge de Arbet, aber blibe wäge de Lüt und em See.
options.en: For work, but I'm staying for the people and the lake.
options.feedback: Lovely answer — and crediting the people will warm any local's heart.
Honest and clear. A little more would make it sing, but this is solid.
A touch dismissive of his town. Even 'work brought me, but I love it' is warmer.

options.es: Doch, scho. Aber mer telefoniered oft, und da han ich au Lüt gärn.
options.en: Yes, I do. But we call often, and I care for people here too.
options.feedback: Honest and heartfelt — and it gently includes his family. Beautifully judged.
Genuine and fine. You can leave it there or add a hopeful note.
Sounds a little cold — most people miss family sometimes, and honesty connects.

steps.speaker: Vater
steps.es: Du bisch da immer willkomme, das weisch, gäll?
steps.en: You're always welcome here, you know that, right?

options.es: Das bedütet mer viel. Merci viilmal.
options.en: That means a lot to me. Thank you very much.
options.feedback: Sincere and moving — exactly how to receive such warmth.
Undersells a heartfelt moment. Let him see it touched you.
